schedules.label: Schedule 3
schedules.note: Pharmacist-controlled medicine. In this list: Colcibra (celecoxib).

schedules.label: Schedule 4
schedules.note: Prescription medicine. In this list: Axolta (rivaroxaban).

schedules.label: Schedule 5
schedules.note: Prescription medicine with stricter controls (often sedating). In this list: Belanex, Dynapayne.

schedules.label: Schedule 6
schedules.note: Tightly controlled (e.g. strong opioids). In this list: Xonoco XR (oxycodone/naloxone).

glossary.def: Medicine that prevents blood clots forming in the veins (DVT) after surgery. Watch for unusual bleeding or bruising.
Non-Steroidal Anti-Inflammatory Drug. Reduces pain AND swelling. Take with food to protect the stomach.
Strong painkiller for moderate-to-severe pain. Can cause drowsiness and constipation. Used short-term and carefully.
Latin 'pro re nata' = take only when needed (here: only if she has pain), not on a fixed schedule.
Must be taken every cycle whether or not there is pain (e.g. the blood thinner and the nerve-pain capsule).
The tablet releases medicine slowly over ~12 hours. Swallow whole – never crush or chew.
Take during or just after a meal to reduce stomach irritation and improve absorption.
Deep Vein Thrombosis – a blood clot in a deep leg vein, a key risk after knee surgery, which the blood thinner prevents.
